title: Reachy Mini reacts to what it sees: a lightweight on-board vision sense detects motion, light, and nearby objects from the camera in realtime on a Raspberry Pi 4 — simple pixel math, no ML — and feeds those events into the behavior/motion system so the robot orients toward what it sees.

claims.text: Reachy Mini reacts to what it sees: a lightweight on-board vision sense detects motion, light, and nearby objects from the camera in realtime on a Raspberry Pi 4 — simple pixel math, no ML — and feeds those events into the behavior/motion system so the robot orients toward what it sees.

honesty_conditions.text: On a Raspberry Pi 4 the full capture->downsample->detect->emit loop sustains >=10 FPS with CPU headroom left for the motion loop.

claims.text: Reachy Mini operators running the on-board CLI, plus the behavior/listen subsystems that consume sense events to drive motion.

honesty_conditions.text: The on-board operator and the listen/motion subsystems are the real consumers — vision events matter to a robot reacting in place, not to a remote dashboard.

claims.text: The robot has a realtime visual sense: it detects motion, brightness/light changes, and best-effort object proximity from the local camera and emits events that orient the head/body toward what it sees.
Today the CLI is sightless — it hears (listen/DoA) and moves, but the on-board camera is unused; perception is audio-only.

honesty_conditions.text: The camera is genuinely unused by the current CLI (no existing vision verb), so this is net-new perception, not a duplicate of something present.

claims.text: Visual reactivity makes the robot feel present and alive, complements audio DoA, and enables gaze-following — without heavy compute, ML, or a GPU.

honesty_conditions.text: A robot that visibly reacts to motion/light reads as more present than one reacting only to sound — the added liveliness is perceptible to a bystander.

claims.text: Not ML: no object recognition, face ID, classification, tracking-by-detection, SLAM or depth mapping; no neural models, no cloud, no GPU. Rudimentary pixel math only.

honesty_conditions.text: Motion and light can be detected usefully with pixel math alone, so dropping ML/GPU does not gut the feature.

claims.text: Local-profile only: frames come from the SDK/IPC local camera path; the pure-HTTP remote profile (camera metadata only, per issue #22) cannot run detection.

honesty_conditions.text: The local SDK/IPC camera path is present whenever the [sdk]/[daemon] extra is installed, so 'local-profile only' still covers the normal on-robot deployment.

claims.text: Runs realtime on a Raspberry Pi 4 (target ~10-15 FPS) within a modest CPU budget on downsampled frames, and a detected motion/light event drives a smooth head orient through the existing serial motion queue (like listen).

honesty_conditions.text: On a Pi 4 the loop holds the target frame rate and a motion/light event yields a visibly smooth head orient within a fraction of a second — measurable on hardware.

claims.text: Detect motion via frame differencing on downsampled grayscale frames (numpy abs-diff + threshold + blob centroid for direction).

honesty_conditions.text: Frame differencing on downsampled grayscale reliably flags real motion (a hand wave at ~1-2 m) while rejecting sensor noise and lighting flicker via a tunable threshold.

claims.text: Detect light via per-frame brightness and bright-region centroid (mean luma + thresholded max region), reacting to changes rather than absolute level.

honesty_conditions.text: A brightness/centroid pass locates the dominant bright region and its direction, distinguishing a real light change from a uniform exposure shift.

claims.text: Expose a 'vision' noun (run/start/stop/restart/status + overview, --json) mirroring 'listen', emitting a Sense the behavior engine / serial motion queue consume to orient the head/body.

honesty_conditions.text: The vision Sense plugs into the same serial motion queue / Sense pattern as listen, so visual orients compose with (do not fight) listen and demo-mode.

claims.text: Build on issue #22's frame-grab substrate (vision specs/status/snapshot via the local SDK/IPC camera path); this feature is the perception+reaction layer (#22's follow-up).
The reachy_mini SDK local camera path (is_local_camera_available) delivers frames fast enough on the Pi 4 to downsample and diff in realtime.

The robot has a realtime visual sense: it detects motion and brightness/light changes from the local camera and emits events that orient the head/body toward what it sees. Object proximity is a parked stretch follow-up.

honesty_conditions.text: Once shipped, waving a hand or moving a light in view turns the head toward it, with no ML model loaded and CPU to spare.

claims.text: Expose a 'vision' noun (run/start/stop/restart/status + overview, --json) that mirrors 'listen': its own loop drives the head/body through the existing serial motion queue. Behavior-engine Sense-channel integration is a follow-up.

honesty_conditions.text: The vision loop drives the same serial motion queue as listen, so visual orients are serialized with (do not fight) listen and demo-mode moves.

claims.text: v1 scope = motion + light via a standalone 'vision' loop mirroring listen (serial motion queue); proximity and behavior-engine Sense-channel integration are explicit follow-ups.

open_vagueness.text: Proximity / 'close objects' detection: method (apparent-size growth or sparse optic-flow) and whether it is feasible at realtime on a Pi 4 — the 'maybe' capability.
Exact FPS / frame resolution / CPU-budget targets, to be measured on real Pi 4 hardware.
